C:
```js
const dedup = a => [...new Set(a)];
```

This goes from **O(n²) to O(n)**. The original calls `a.indexOf(x)` once per element, and each `indexOf` scans the array — so for an array of length n you do roughly n² comparisons. `Set` builds a hash-based lookup, so each insert is amortized O(1) and the whole thing is linear.

Concretely, at n = 10,000 the original does ~100M comparisons; the Set version does ~10,000 insertions. Both preserve first-seen order.

One behavioral note: `Set` uses SameValueZero equality, so it correctly dedupes `NaN` (treats `NaN` as equal to itself), whereas the `indexOf` version keeps every `NaN` because `NaN === NaN` is false. For all other values the results are identical.
